compiled_state.title: Implementation-plan re-invocation safety for in-flight ticket graphs
compiled_state.author: Claude
compiled_state.description:
## Context
DSO practitioners have no structured path back to planning when implementation reveals the plan is wrong. Today, re-invoking implementation-plan on an epic or story that already has children creates duplicate tasks and has no awareness of closed work. Brainstorm and preplanning are similarly unsafe to re-invoke mid-implementation. This epic makes the planning skills safe to re-invoke with in-flight ticket graphs — the foundational mechanics that the sprint detection layer (separate epic) will build on.

## Success Criteria
1. Implementation-plan safely handles re-invocation with an in-flight ticket graph: on entry it reads all children (including archived via --include-archived), classifies each as closed (read-only — never reopened, duplicated, or modified), in-progress (flagged for review), or open (candidate for revision). It produces only new tasks or reopened tasks.
2. When implementation-plan cannot produce a diff plan that satisfies the parent epic or story success criteria — because the criteria themselves are ambiguous or contradicted by current codebase state — it emits REPLAN_ESCALATE: brainstorm with an explanation of the ambiguity. The calling orchestrator is responsible for routing this signal.
3. After brainstorm revises the epic, preplanning re-runs (it already handles re-invocation with existing closed stories), then implementation-plan re-runs to realign tasks to potentially revised parent stories. The cascade terminates when implementation-plan produces a plan it can execute with confidence, or after sprint.max_replan_cycles (configurable in dso-config.conf, default 2) cascade iterations — whichever comes first.
4. At epic validation before closure, the same routing applies: if a failing success criterion's text does not match what the implementation actually does (intent gap), route to brainstorm with user confirmation. If the criterion is clear but implementation does not fully satisfy it (implementation gap), route to implementation-plan in epic-direct mode to create new tasks. The distinction is made by comparing the criterion text against actual codebase behavior.

## Dependencies
None — this is a foundational epic that other epics depend on.

## Approach
Implementation-plan gains a re-invocation guard at entry that reads the ticket graph and classifies children. It produces diff plans (new + reopened tasks only). A REPLAN_ESCALATE signal is added to its output protocol for brainstorm escalation. The post-brainstorm cascade flows through preplanning and implementation-plan with a configurable cycle cap.

## Scenario Analysis
- Infinite re-planning loop (critical): Addressed by max_replan_cycles cap (SC3)
- Sub-agent guard blocks re-invocation (high): Re-invocation must run at orchestrator level via Skill tool, not inside a sub-agent context
- Stale preplanning context file after brainstorm cascade (high): Cascade must invalidate context file before preplanning re-run
- Archived tickets missed by guard (medium): Addressed by --include-archived in SC1
- Failure counter reset on new task ID (high): Failure tracking should be per story, not per task ID

### Planning Intelligence Log
- Web research (Step 2.6): not triggered
  - Bright-line conditions that fired: none
- Scenario analysis (Step 2.75): triggered
  - Scenarios surviving blue team filter: 9 (from parent epic analysis, filtered to this scope)
- Practitioner-requested additional cycles: none

comments.body: Converted from original ticket 1954-72a8 (Implementation Escape Hatch). Split into two epics: this one (re-invocation safety mechanics) and 9d3e-957d (sprint detection and routing). This epic is the foundational layer.
comments.author: Claude

comments.body:
## SC4 Clarification (added after implementation)

The original SC4 text reads: 'route to implementation-plan in epic-direct mode to create new tasks.'

After implementation, the actual behavior is:

**SC4 (as implemented)**: At epic validation before closure, if a failing success criterion's text does not match what the implementation actually does (intent gap), route to brainstorm with user confirmation. If the criterion is clear but implementation does not fully satisfy it (implementation gap), route to **Phase 7 Step 1 bug-task creation** — the gap-classification sub-agent emits ROUTING: implementation-plan as a routing signal label, and the sprint orchestrator responds by creating targeted bug tasks via the existing Phase 7 remediation flow. The orchestrator does NOT invoke /dso:implementation-plan as a separate skill for implementation_gap resolution. The distinction between intent_gap and implementation_gap is made by the gap-classification sub-agent (see plugins/dso/docs/contracts/gap-classification-output.md) by comparing the criterion text against actual codebase behavior.

This clarification reflects option (b) from the original epic design: revise the SC language to match the actual behavior rather than changing the implementation to invoke /dso:implementation-plan in epic-direct mode.
comments.author: Test
